title: Affine functions
text: Suppose that A ‚ąąRm√ón and b ‚ąąRm. Show that the function f (x) = Ax + b is affine.

title: Affine functions
text:
Let x, y ‚ąąRn and őĪ, ő≤ ‚ąąR with őĪ + ő≤ = 1
f(őĪx + ő≤y) = A(őĪx + ő≤y) + b
= A(őĪx) + A(ő≤y) + (őĪ + ő≤)b
= őĪAx + ő≤Ay + őĪb + ő≤b
= őĪ(Ax + b) + ő≤(Ay + b)
= őĪf(x) + ő≤f(y)
Hence f(x) is affine.

title: Affine functions
text:
Problem 1(a):
Suppose that A ‚ąąRm√ón and b ‚ąąRm. Show that the function f (x) = Ax + b is affine.

Solution:
Let x, y ‚ąąRn and őĪ, ő≤ ‚ąąR with őĪ + ő≤ = 1
f(őĪx + ő≤y) = A(őĪx + ő≤y) + b
= A(őĪx) + A(ő≤y) + (őĪ + ő≤)b
= őĪAx + ő≤Ay + őĪb + ő≤b
= őĪ(Ax + b) + ő≤(Ay + b)
= őĪf(x) + ő≤f(y)
Hence f(x) is affine.

title: Affine functions
text:
Prove the converse, namely, show that any affine function f can be represented
uniquely as f (x) = Ax + b for some A ‚ąąRm√ón and b ‚ąąRm.

title: Affine functions
text:
Define g(x) = f(x) ‚ąíf(0). We will show that g(x) is linear by using the fact that
f(x) is affine. Let x, y ‚ąąRn and őĪ ‚ąąR
g(őĪx) = f(őĪx) ‚ąíf(0)
= f(őĪx + (1 ‚ąíőĪ)0) ‚ąíf(0)
= őĪf(x) + (1 ‚ąíőĪ)f(0) ‚ąíf(0)
f is affine

= őĪg(x)

So far we have shown g is a linear mapping. In lecture, we have learned that if g
is linear, then g(x) can be represented as g(x) = Ax for some A ‚ąąRm√ón (for the
choice of canonical basis of Rn, Rm). Now we show that such A is unique.
Suppose there exists A, B ‚ąąRm√ón such that g(x) = Ax, g(x) = Bx, we have:
0 = g(x) ‚ąíg(x) = (A ‚ąíB)x
‚áí0 = (A ‚ąíB)x
Because the last equation must be satisfied by all x ‚ąąRn, we must have A = B
(note that in this case N(A ‚ąíB) = Rn, and thus R(A ‚ąíB) = 0 which implies
A ‚ąíB = 0).

Hence, for any linear function g : Rn ‚ÜíRm, g can be uniquely represented as
g(x) = Ax for some A ‚ąąRm√ón. Letting b := f(0) ‚ąąRm, any affine function f
satisfies:
f(x) ‚ąíb = Ax ‚áíf(x) = Ax + b
To show that b is also unique, suppose there exists b1, b2 such that f(x) = Ax+b1,
f(x) = Ax + b2, we have
f(0) = b1 = b2
Hence, f(x) = Ax + b for some unique A ‚ąąRm√ón and b ‚ąąRm.

f(0) = b1 = b2
Hence, f(x) = Ax + b for some unique A ‚ąąRm√ón and b ‚ąąRm.

title: Linear Maps and Differentiation of polynomials
text:
Consider the transformation T : Pn ‚ÜíPn defined by
T(p(x)) = dp(x)
dx
.
For example, T(1 + 3x + x2) = 3 + 2x. Show that T is linear.

title: Linear Maps and Differentiation of polynomials
text:
Let p(x), q(x) ‚ąąPn and őĪ, ő≤ ‚ąąR
T(őĪp(x) + ő≤q(x)) = d
dx(őĪp(x) + ő≤q(x))
= d
dx(őĪp(x)) + d
dx(ő≤q(x))
= őĪdp(x)
dx
+ ő≤ dq(x)
dx
= őĪT(p(x)) + ő≤T(q(x))
Hence T is a linear function.

Hence T is a linear function.

A ‚ąąR(n+1)√ó(n+1). Find the rank of A.

title: Rank of AAT
text:
Suppose F = R. Either prove that rank(AAT)=rank(A) or provide a counterexam-
ple.

title: Rank of AAT
text:
F = R : Let x ‚ąąN(A‚ä§), then A‚ä§x = 0, and AA‚ä§x = 0, therefore x ‚ąąN(AA‚ä§).
Thus, N(A‚ä§) ‚äÜN(AA‚ä§), and dim(N(A‚ä§)) ‚Č§dim(N(AA‚ä§)). By rank-nullity
theorem we have
rank(AA‚ä§) ‚Č§rank(A‚ä§).
(1)
Let x ‚ąąN(AA‚ä§), then AA‚ä§x = 0, and x‚ä§AA‚ä§x = ‚ü®A‚ä§x, A‚ä§x‚ü©= 0 where
‚ü®a, b‚ü©= b‚ä§a is a valid inner product of Rn over the field R. By the properties of the

inner product, this occurs iff A‚ä§x = 0, i.e., x ‚ąąN(A‚ä§). Thus, N(AA‚ä§) ‚äÜN(A‚ä§),
and dim(N(AA‚ä§)) ‚Č§dim(N(A‚ä§)). By rank-nullity theorem we have
rank(A‚ä§) ‚Č§rank(AA‚ä§).
(2)
From (1) and (2), we have rank(A‚ä§) = rank(AA‚ä§).
Since rank(A‚ä§) = rank(A), then rank(AA‚ä§) = rank(A).

title: Rank of AAT
text:
Problem 3(a):
Suppose F = R. Either prove that rank(AAT)=rank(A) or provide a counterexam-
ple.

Solution:
F = R : Let x ‚ąąN(A‚ä§), then A‚ä§x = 0, and AA‚ä§x = 0, therefore x ‚ąąN(AA‚ä§).
Thus, N(A‚ä§) ‚äÜN(AA‚ä§), and dim(N(A‚ä§)) ‚Č§dim(N(AA‚ä§)). By rank-nullity
theorem we have
rank(AA‚ä§) ‚Č§rank(A‚ä§).
(1)
Let x ‚ąąN(AA‚ä§), then AA‚ä§x = 0, and x‚ä§AA‚ä§x = ‚ü®A‚ä§x, A‚ä§x‚ü©= 0 where
‚ü®a, b‚ü©= b‚ä§a is a valid inner product of Rn over the field R. By the properties of the

inner product, this occurs iff A‚ä§x = 0, i.e., x ‚ąąN(A‚ä§). Thus, N(AA‚ä§) ‚äÜN(A‚ä§),
and dim(N(AA‚ä§)) ‚Č§dim(N(A‚ä§)). By rank-nullity theorem we have
rank(A‚ä§) ‚Č§rank(AA‚ä§).
(2)
From (1) and (2), we have rank(A‚ä§) = rank(AA‚ä§).
Since rank(A‚ä§) = rank(A), then rank(AA‚ä§) = rank(A).

rank(AA‚ä§) = 0 Őł= rank(A).

If A ‚ąąFm√ón,B ‚ąąFn√ók then
rank(B) ‚Č§rank(AB) + dim(null(A))

A ‚ąąFm√ón, B ‚ąąFn√ók
The range space of B denoted R(B) is a subspace of Fn.
Restrict the linear map A to R(B) i.e. consider new linear map f : R(B) ‚ÜíFm
given by f(x) = Ax for all vectors x in R(B).
Domain of f is R(B) and hence dimension of domain is rank(B).
R(f) = {Ay : y ‚ąąR(B)} = {ABx : x ‚ąąFk} = R(AB)
N(f) = {y ‚ąąR(B) : Ay = 0} = N(A) ‚ą©R(B)
We can apply Rank-Nullity Theorem on this new map f.
rank(B) = dim(R(f)) + dim(N(f))
= rank(AB) + dim(N(A) ‚ą©R(B))
‚Č§rank(AB) + dim(N(A))

Problem 4(a):
If A ‚ąąFm√ón,B ‚ąąFn√ók then
rank(B) ‚Č§rank(AB) + dim(null(A))

Solution:
A ‚ąąFm√ón, B ‚ąąFn√ók
The range space of B denoted R(B) is a subspace of Fn.
Restrict the linear map A to R(B) i.e. consider new linear map f : R(B) ‚ÜíFm
given by f(x) = Ax for all vectors x in R(B).
Domain of f is R(B) and hence dimension of domain is rank(B).
R(f) = {Ay : y ‚ąąR(B)} = {ABx : x ‚ąąFk} = R(AB)
N(f) = {y ‚ąąR(B) : Ay = 0} = N(A) ‚ą©R(B)
We can apply Rank-Nullity Theorem on this new map f.
rank(B) = dim(R(f)) + dim(N(f))
= rank(AB) + dim(N(A) ‚ą©R(B))
‚Č§rank(AB) + dim(N(A))

If A ‚ąąFm√ón,B ‚ąąFm√ón then
rank(A + B) ‚Č§rank(A) + rank(B)

Problem 4(b):
If A ‚ąąFm√ón,B ‚ąąFm√ón then
rank(A + B) ‚Č§rank(A) + rank(B)

Solution:
A ‚ąąFm√ón, B ‚ąąFm√ón
R(A + B) = {(A + B)x : x ‚ąąFn}
= {Ax + Bx : x ‚ąąFn}
‚äÜ{Ax : x ‚ąąFn} + {Bx : x ‚ąąFn}
= R(A) + R(B)
Hence rank(A + B) ‚Č§dim(R(A) + R(B))
Let B1, B2 be basis for R(A) and R(B) respectively. Then B1 ‚ą™B2 spans R(A) +
R(B). Hence dim(R(A) + R(B)) ‚Č§rank(A) + rank(B).
Hence rank(A + B) ‚Č§rank(A) + rank(B)

Suppose A, B ‚ąąFm√óm. Then show that if AB = 0 then
rank(A) + rank(B) ‚Č§m

Let A, B ‚ąąFm√óm. From part (a),
rank(B) ‚Č§rank(AB) + dim(N(A))
= dim(N(A))
= m ‚ąírank(A)
[‚ąĶRank Nullity for A]
Hence rank(A) + rank(B) ‚Č§m

Problem 4(c):
Suppose A, B ‚ąąFm√óm. Then show that if AB = 0 then
rank(A) + rank(B) ‚Č§m

Solution:
Let A, B ‚ąąFm√óm. From part (a),
rank(B) ‚Č§rank(AB) + dim(N(A))
= dim(N(A))
= m ‚ąírank(A)
[‚ąĶRank Nullity for A]
Hence rank(A) + rank(B) ‚Č§m

Suppose A ‚ąąFm√óm. Then show A2 = A if and only if
rank(A) + rank(A ‚ąíI) = m
where I ‚ąąFm√óm is the identity matrix.

Let A ‚ąąFm√óm
Need to show A2 = A ‚áź‚áírank(A) + rank(A ‚ąíI) = m
( =‚áí) Given A2 = A
From part (b),
m = rank(I) = rank(A + (I ‚ąíA)) ‚Č§rank(A) + rank(I ‚ąíA)
giving rank(A) + rank(I ‚ąíA) ‚Č•m.
From part (c),
rank(A) + rank(I ‚ąíA) ‚Č§m
Hence m = rank(A) + rank(I ‚ąíA).
As rank(I ‚ąíA) = rank(A ‚ąíI), we get m = rank(A) + rank(A ‚ąíI)
( ‚áź= ) Given rank(A) + rank(A ‚ąíI) = m
Let x ‚ąąN(A), then (A ‚ąíI)(‚ąíx) = ‚ąíAx + Ix = x.
Hence N(A) ‚äÜR(A ‚ąíI).
As dim(N(A)) = m ‚ąírank(A) = rank(A ‚ąíI) we get N(A) = R(A ‚ąíI).
R(A(A ‚ąíI)) = {A(A ‚ąíI)x : x ‚ąąFm} = {Ay : y ‚ąąR(A ‚ąíI)} = {0}
Hence A(A ‚ąíI) = 0 i.e. A2 = A.

Problem 4(d):
Suppose A ‚ąąFm√óm. Then show A2 = A if and only if
rank(A) + rank(A ‚ąíI) = m
where I ‚ąąFm√óm is the identity matrix.

Solution:
Let A ‚ąąFm√óm
Need to show A2 = A ‚áź‚áírank(A) + rank(A ‚ąíI) = m
( =‚áí) Given A2 = A
From part (b),
m = rank(I) = rank(A + (I ‚ąíA)) ‚Č§rank(A) + rank(I ‚ąíA)
giving rank(A) + rank(I ‚ąíA) ‚Č•m.
From part (c),
rank(A) + rank(I ‚ąíA) ‚Č§m
Hence m = rank(A) + rank(I ‚ąíA).
As rank(I ‚ąíA) = rank(A ‚ąíI), we get m = rank(A) + rank(A ‚ąíI)
( ‚áź= ) Given rank(A) + rank(A ‚ąíI) = m
Let x ‚ąąN(A), then (A ‚ąíI)(‚ąíx) = ‚ąíAx + Ix = x.
Hence N(A) ‚äÜR(A ‚ąíI).
As dim(N(A)) = m ‚ąírank(A) = rank(A ‚ąíI) we get N(A) = R(A ‚ąíI).
R(A(A ‚ąíI)) = {A(A ‚ąíI)x : x ‚ąąFm} = {Ay : y ‚ąąR(A ‚ąíI)} = {0}
Hence A(A ‚ąíI) = 0 i.e. A2 = A.
rank(A) = n, and rank(B) = m.

rank(A) = n, rank(B) = m

Now, as B is a n √ó m matrix and rank(B) = m, by the rank-nullity theorem we can
say that dim(N(B)) = m ‚ąírank(B) = 0. Therefore, the null space of B will be the
trivial subspace.

Also, as it is given that n ‚Č•m and rank(B) = m, therefore dim(Rn) ‚Č•dim(R(B)).
Hence in the general case, it is not guaranteed that R(B) will span all of Rn. There-
fore, for this case, we can conclude the following:

Problem 5(a):
rank(A) = n, and rank(B) = m.

therefore from lecture discussions, we know that there exists a unique inverse matrix

(5)
Now, as B is a n √ó m matrix and rank(B) = m, by the rank-nullity theorem we can
say that dim(N(B)) = m ‚ąírank(B) = 0. Therefore, the null space of B will be the
trivial subspace.

Also, as it is given that n ‚Č•m and rank(B) = m, therefore dim(Rn) ‚Č•dim(R(B)).
Hence in the general case, it is not guaranteed that R(B) will span all of Rn. There-
fore, for this case, we can conclude the following:

title: Infinite Dimensional Vector Spaces
text:
Is there a vector in S which can be represented as a finite linear combination of other
vectors in S?

title: Infinite Dimensional Vector Spaces
text:
Let assume there exists (x+i0)i0 ‚ąąS that can be represented as a linear combination
of k other vectors in S:
(x + i0)i0 =
k

for a set of k non-zero scalars {őĪ1, őĪ2, ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , őĪk} ‚äāR and i1 < i2 < ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ < ik and i0 Őł= ij
for 1 ‚Č§j ‚Č§k
There are two cases:

‚ÄĘ Suppose i0 < ik, we can rewrite the eq.10 as

In this equation the left hand side (LHS) has a higher degree than the right
hand side (RHS), since the coefficient of xik on LHS is 1 and on the RHS is 0.
Thus there does not exist any set of {őĪ1, őĪ2, ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , őĪk}, and {i0, i1, ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , ik} that
this equality hold for all x ‚ąą[0, 1].
‚ÄĘ Now suppose, i0 > ik. Then LHS of eq.10 has a term xi0 which does not occur
on the RHS of eq.10 as ij < i0 for all 1 ‚Č§j ‚Č§k, thus the coefficient of xi0 on
LHS is 1 and on the RHS is 0. Similar to the first case, there does not exist any
set of {őĪ1, őĪ2, ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , őĪk}, and {i0, i1, ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , ik} that eq.10 hold for all x ‚ąą[0, 1].
This is a contradiction.
Hence no vector in S can be written as a finite linear
combination of the other vectors in S.

title: Infinite Dimensional Vector Spaces
text:
Can any vector in C0([0, 1]) be represented as a finite linear combination of vectors
in S?
[Finite linear combination is a linear combination with finite number of terms]

title: Infinite Dimensional Vector Spaces
text:
Finite linear combinations of vectors in S always results in polynomials of finite
degree. However, C0([0, 1]) contains other functions such as ex, |x ‚ąí0.5| which are
continuous but are not equal to any finite degree polynomial and hence cannot be
represented as finite linear combinations of vectors in S.
Suppose ex =

mial with degree less than l is 0.
Hence ex cannot be represented as a finite linear combination of vectors in S.
